title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: glmOJ provides streamlined workflow fitting, diagnosing, interpreting count regression models. four supported families : general-purpose wrapper countGLM() fits four selects best user-chosen criterion (decide): "BIC" (default), "AIC", "LogLik", "McFadden" (McFadden pseudo-R²).

what: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: Greenberg et al. (2026) investigate environmental social factors influence EPA criminal prosecutions occur across 3,143 US counties (2011–2020). response variable FinalEC count criminal prosecutions per county.
code: data("Greenberg26.dat")

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: fitting, summarizeCountData() gives quick numerical graphical overview count response alongside predictor.
code: summarizeCountData(   FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc +     pctnonwhite10 +     metro +     gdp2017b +     fac_penalty_count +     CIDDist +     EPAregion,   data = Greenberg26.dat ) #> $summary #>        mean       var var_mean_ratio n_zero n_total #> 1 0.2356564 0.6490525       2.754232   2654    3085 #>  #> $counts #>    count freq #> 1      0 2654 #> 2      1  299 #> 3      2   66 #> 4      3   31 #> 5      4   14 #> 6      5    5 #> 7      6    6 #> 8      7    3 #> 9      8    3 #> 10     9    2 #> 11    11    1 #> 12    12    1 #>  #> $plot #>  #> $pairs_plot

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: first fit Poisson GLM Overall Environmental Quality Index demographic/geographic controls.
code: mod.pois <- poissonGLM(   FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc +     pctnonwhite10 +     metro +     gdp2017b +     fac_penalty_count +     CIDDist +     EPAregion,   data = Greenberg26.dat )

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions > 2. Poisson Regression
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions > 2. Poisson Regression
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: dispersion ratio 1.615 flagged red — observed variance ~60% larger expected Poisson, suggesting overdispersion. also inspect squared Pearson residual plot:  wedge shape confirms mean-variance relationship well captured Poisson assumption.
code: mod.pois$diagnostics$plot mod.pois$diagnostics$r2_plot

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: negative binomial adds free dispersion parameter θ\theta handle overdispersion.
code: mod.nb <- negbinGLM(   FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc +     pctnonwhite10 +     metro +     gdp2017b +     fac_penalty_count +     CIDDist +     EPAregion,   data = Greenberg26.dat,   control = stats::glm.control(maxit = 100) )

title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions > 3. Negative Binomial Regression
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: dispersion ratio now 1.021 — much closer 1. estimated θ\theta 0.649.
code: mod.nb$diagnostics$plot mod.nb$diagnostics$r2_plot

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: Poisson model nested within negative binomial (Poisson NB θ→∞\theta \\infty), can use likelihood ratio test. underlying glm/glm.nb fit objects available via $model: negative binomial model significantly better (p<0.0001p < 0.0001), confirming overdispersion genuine problem Poisson fit.
code: lmtest::lrtest(mod.pois$model, mod.nb$model) #> Likelihood ratio test #>  #> Model 1: FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc + pctnonwhite10 + metro + gdp2017b +  #>     fac_penalty_count + CIDDist + EPAregion #> Model 2: FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc + pctnonwhite10 + metro + gdp2017b +  #>     fac_penalty_count + CIDDist + EPAregion #>   #Df  LogLik Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)     #> 1  16 -1573.7                          #> 2  17 -1465.2  1 217.13  < 2.2e-16 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: Rather fitting model manually, countGLM() fits four families selects best criterion specified decide (default "BIC") — arriving conclusion automatically.

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions > 5. Using the countGLM Wrapper
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: wrapper selects winner manual LRT. Individual fits remain accessible: result1$fits$negbin, result1$fits$poisson, etc.
code: result1 <- countGLM(   FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc +     pctnonwhite10 +     metro +     gdp2017b +     fac_penalty_count +     CIDDist +     EPAregion,   data = Greenberg26.dat ) print(result1) #>  #> Call: #> countGLM(formula = FinalEC ~ eqi_2jan2018_vc + pctnonwhite10 +  #>     metro + gdp2017b + fac_penalty_count + CIDDist + EPAregion,  #>     data = Greenberg26.dat) #>  #> Model comparison (sorted by BIC (ascending)): #>             model     AIC     BIC #>   zeroinfl_negbin 2845.16 3044.30 #>            negbin 2964.32 3066.90 #>  zeroinfl_poisson 2920.58 3113.67 #>           poisson 3179.45 3276.00 #>  #> Selected model: zeroinfl_negbin #>  #> Recommendation: #>   Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial was selected by BIC (BIC = 3044.30). #>   The Poisson dispersion ratio is 1.62 (> 1.5), indicating #>   overdispersion. DHARMa zero-inflation test is significant for the #>   Poisson fit (p = 0.000) but not for the Negative Binomial fit (p = #>   0.654); excess zeros may be explained by overdispersion alone.

previous_headings: Case Study: Federal Environmental Crime Prosecutions > 5. Using the countGLM Wrapper
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: researchers also evaluated whether separate Water, Air, Land, Socioeconomic indices informative composite Overall EQI. countGLM handles one call: negative binomial selected. non-nested comparison two winning models (overall EQI vs sub-indices) can done Vuong test via nonnest2::vuongtest(result1$fits$negbin$model, result2$fits$negbin$model).
code: result2 <- countGLM(   FinalEC ~ water_eqi_2jan2018_vc +     land_eqi_2jan2018_vc +     air_eqi_2jan2018_vc +     sociod_eqi_2jan2018_vc +     pctnonwhite10 +     metro +     gdp2017b +     fac_penalty_count +     CIDDist +     EPAregion,   data = Greenberg26.dat ) print(result2) #>  #> Call: #> countGLM(formula = FinalEC ~ water_eqi_2jan2018_vc + land_eqi_2jan2018_vc +  #>     air_eqi_2jan2018_vc + sociod_eqi_2jan2018_vc + pctnonwhite10 +  #>     metro + gdp2017b + fac_penalty_count + CIDDist + EPAregion,  #>     data = Greenberg26.dat) #>  #> Model comparison (sorted by BIC (ascending)): #>             model     AIC     BIC #>            negbin 2953.72 3074.41 #>   zeroinfl_negbin 2856.57 3091.91 #>  zeroinfl_poisson 2933.58 3162.89 #>           poisson 3144.98 3259.64 #>  #> Selected model: negbin #>  #> Recommendation: #>   Negative Binomial was selected by BIC (BIC = 3074.41). The Poisson #>   dispersion ratio is 1.49, consistent with equidispersion. DHARMa #>   zero-inflation test is significant for the Poisson fit (p = 0.000) #>   but not for the Negative Binomial fit (p = 0.828); excess zeros may #>   be explained by overdispersion alone.

title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: interpret_coef() translates exponentiated coefficient plain-language statement, 95% CI automatic note predictor discernible zero (p > 0.05).

text: Pass countGLM result directly — delegates best-fitting model:
code: interpret_coef(result2, "pctnonwhite10") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in pctnonwhite10 is associated with a 1.4% increase in the expected count of FinalEC (exp(β) = 1.014, 95% CI: [1.008, 1.020]).

text: zero-inflated models, use component = "count" (default) component = "zero":
code: interpret_coef(zi_fit, "pctnonwhite10", component = "count") interpret_coef(zi_fit, "pctnonwhite10", component = "zero")

what: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: Dahir (2025) models number surveillance cameras per census tract across ten US cities. response cam_count non-negative integer 87.6% zeros variance--mean ratio approximately 3.6 — classic signs excess zeros overdispersion.
code: data("Dahir25.dat")

previous_headings: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: summary table makes issue concrete: variance--mean ratio far exceeds 1 frequency table dominated zeros. Camera placement varies substantially land-use zone — mixed industrial tracts cameras roughly 28–42% cases, residential tracts (majority, n = 8913) cameras fewer 10%. mixture structurally camera-free tracts alongside genuinely high-count commercial zones exactly setting zero-inflated overdispersed count models needed.

previous_headings: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: may want start manually fitting Poisson model confirm overdispersion zero-inflation diagnostics. formula includes quadratic term predictor allow non-linear effects, road length included offset: DHARMa zero-inflation test result:
code: pois.cam <- poissonGLM(   cam_count ~ pnhwht +     pnhblk +     entropy_rank +     total_crime_rate +     modal_zone +     pop +     hinc +     pvac +     mhmval +     city +     offset(log_road_length) +     I(pnhwht^2) +     I(pnhblk^2) +     I(entropy_rank^2),   data = Dahir25.dat ) print(pois.cam) #>  #> Call: #> poissonGLM(formula = cam_count ~ pnhwht + pnhblk + entropy_rank +  #>     total_crime_rate + modal_zone + pop + hinc + pvac + mhmval +  #>     city + offset(log_road_length) + I(pnhwht^2) + I(pnhblk^2) +  #>     I(entropy_rank^2), data = Dahir25.dat) #>  #> Model family: poissonGLM  #>  #> Coefficients (on response scale): #>                   term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>            (Intercept)   0.1337   0.1055   0.1695  0.0000   *** #>                 pnhwht   0.9634   0.8968   1.0349  0.3067       #>                 pnhblk   0.8723   0.8106   0.9387  0.0003   *** #>           entropy_rank   1.4478   0.7754   2.7031  0.2454       #>       total_crime_rate   1.0908   1.0796   1.1022  0.0000   *** #>   modal_zoneindustrial   1.1534   0.9558   1.3917  0.1366       #>        modal_zonemixed   1.2535   1.0415   1.5087  0.0168     * #>       modal_zonepublic   0.5876   0.4649   0.7428  0.0000   *** #>  modal_zoneresidential   0.4298   0.3742   0.4937  0.0000   *** #>        modal_zoneroads   0.6960   0.4447   1.0895  0.1130       #>                    pop   1.1213   1.0990   1.1442  0.0000   *** #>                   hinc   0.7716   0.7305   0.8149  0.0000   *** #>                   pvac   1.1807   1.1373   1.2258  0.0000   *** #>                 mhmval   1.1586   1.1036   1.2164  0.0000   *** #>             cityBoston   1.2368   1.0563   1.4481  0.0083    ** #>            cityChicago   0.1851   0.1546   0.2216  0.0000   *** #>        cityLos Angeles   0.0495   0.0399   0.0615  0.0000   *** #>          cityMilwaukee   0.3249   0.2688   0.3927  0.0000   *** #>           cityNew York   0.2073   0.1776   0.2421  0.0000   *** #>       cityPhiladelphia   0.4482   0.3808   0.5274  0.0000   *** #>      citySan Francisco   0.6071   0.5090   0.7241  0.0000   *** #>            citySeattle   0.1768   0.1440   0.2171  0.0000   *** #>         cityWashington   0.4568   0.3008   0.6937  0.0002   *** #>            I(pnhwht^2)   1.0487   0.9979   1.1020  0.0607     . #>            I(pnhblk^2)   1.0139   0.9881   1.0402  0.2939       #>      I(entropy_rank^2)   1.2363   0.7094   2.1546  0.4541       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 1.8401 #> AIC: 11299.96 zi <- pois.cam$diagnostics$zi_test cat(sprintf(   "DHARMa zero-inflation test: p = %.4f  |  Detected: %s\n",   zi$p_value,   zi$detected )) #> DHARMa zero-inflation test: p = 0.0000  |  Detected: TRUE zi$plot

previous_headings: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: countGLM() fits four count families selects best criterion decide (default "BIC"). Road length (log_road_length) included offset count component; ziformula = NULL, automatically applied zero-inflation component well — countGLM prints note confirming : comparison table shows AIC BIC four families, sorted selection criterion. selected model zeroinfl_negbin. recommendation captures relevant dispersion zero-inflation diagnostics automatically, explaining family preferred.
code: result_cam <- countGLM(   cam_count ~ pnhblk +     pnhwht +     total_crime_rate +     hinc +     pvac +     modal_zone +     offset(log_road_length),   data = Dahir25.dat ) print(result_cam) #>  #> Call: #> countGLM(formula = cam_count ~ pnhblk + pnhwht + total_crime_rate +  #>     hinc + pvac + modal_zone + offset(log_road_length), data = Dahir25.dat) #>  #> Model comparison (sorted by BIC (ascending)): #>             model      AIC      BIC #>   zeroinfl_negbin 11082.49 11251.78 #>            negbin 11306.16 11394.49 #>  zeroinfl_poisson 11791.50 11953.43 #>           poisson 13201.10 13282.07 #>  #> Selected model: zeroinfl_negbin #>  #> Recommendation: #>   Zero-Inflated Negative Binomial was selected by BIC (BIC = 11251.78). #>   The Poisson dispersion ratio is 2.42 (> 1.5), indicating #>   overdispersion. DHARMa zero-inflation test is significant for the #>   Poisson fit (p = 0.000) but not for the Negative Binomial fit (p = #>   0.542); excess zeros may be explained by overdispersion alone.

previous_headings: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: four model families share assumption predictors strongly collinear. countGLM() automatically computes Variance Inflation Factors stores $vif. avoid false positives structural collinearity — expected whenever interaction polynomial terms present — VIF computed OLS model retains order-1 (main-effect) terms formula. Offset terms excluded well. VIF = 1 means predictor uncorrelated others; values ~5 generally acceptable. VIF > 5 triggers warning. predictors well threshold, confirming multicollinearity concern model. illustrate interaction-term handling, fitting Poisson model quadratic term inflate VIF underlying predictors:
code: result_cam$vif #>                pnhblk                pnhwht      total_crime_rate  #>              1.696259              2.292616              1.152382  #>                  hinc                  pvac  modal_zoneindustrial  #>              1.592488              1.093179              1.115794  #>       modal_zonemixed      modal_zonepublic modal_zoneresidential  #>              1.101121              1.151036              1.495288  #>       modal_zoneroads  #>              1.145420 result_quad <- suppressWarnings(countGLM(   cam_count ~ pnhblk +     pnhwht +     total_crime_rate +     I(total_crime_rate^2) +     offset(log_road_length),   data = Dahir25.dat )) result_quad$vif # only main-effect terms: pnhblk, pnhwht, total_crime_rate #>                pnhblk                pnhwht      total_crime_rate  #>              1.683595              1.637775              1.726555  #> I(total_crime_rate^2)  #>              1.648745

previous_headings: Case Study 2: Urban Surveillance Camera Counts (Dahir 2025)
title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: interpret_coef() delegates best-fitting model automatically: zero-inflated models, count zero components can interpreted separately. count component describes expected camera count among tracts counting process; zero component describes odds structural zero (tract never receives camera):
code: interpret_coef(result_cam, "total_crime_rate") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in total_crime_rate is associated with a 15.7% increase in the expected rate of ~pnhblk + pnhwht + total_crime_rate + hinc + pvac + modal_zone +  per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 1.157, 95% CI: [1.098, 1.219]). Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in total_crime_rate is associated with a 15.7% increase in the expected rate of     offset(log_road_length) per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 1.157, 95% CI: [1.098, 1.219]). interpret_coef(result_cam, "hinc") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in hinc is associated with a 10.8% decrease in the expected rate of ~pnhblk + pnhwht + total_crime_rate + hinc + pvac + modal_zone +  per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 0.892, 95% CI: [0.807, 0.985]). Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in hinc is associated with a 10.8% decrease in the expected rate of     offset(log_road_length) per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 0.892, 95% CI: [0.807, 0.985]). interpret_coef(result_cam, "total_crime_rate", component = "count") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in total_crime_rate is associated with a 15.7% increase in the expected rate of ~pnhblk + pnhwht + total_crime_rate + hinc + pvac + modal_zone +  per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 1.157, 95% CI: [1.098, 1.219]). Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in total_crime_rate is associated with a 15.7% increase in the expected rate of     offset(log_road_length) per unit of exposure (exp(β) = 1.157, 95% CI: [1.098, 1.219]). interpret_coef(result_cam, "total_crime_rate", component = "zero") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in total_crime_rate is associated with a 79.1% decrease in the odds of being a structural zero (exp(β) = 0.209, 95% CI: [0.144, 0.304]).

title: glmOJ: Count Regression Modeling
text: Dahir, Abdi Latif. 2025. “Surveillance Camera Placement Urban Inequality.” Working paper. Greenberg, Pierce, Erik W Johnson, Jennifer Schwartz, Rylie Wartinger. 2026. “Social Factors Shape Federal Environmental Crime Prosecution Patterns USA.” Nature Sustainability, 1–5.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Oscar Jaroker. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Jaroker O (2026). glmOJ: Toolkit Count Regression Modeling. R package version 0.0.0.9000, https://github.com/ojaroker/glmOJ.
code: @Manual{,   title = {glmOJ: Toolkit for Count Regression Modeling},   author = {Oscar Jaroker},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/ojaroker/glmOJ}, }

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: glmOJ R package provides structured workflow count regression modeling. wraps glm, MASS::glm.nb, pscl::zeroinfl fit, diagnose, interpret count regression models, output reported response scale rather link scale.

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: Count data — non-negative integers like number events, species, visits — require specialized regression models. glmOJ guides analyst four steps: Summarize response variable relationship predictors modeling. Fit one count regression models supported families. Assess model conditions using randomized quantile residuals (RQR) Pearson dispersion ratio. Interpret output response scale (exponentiated coefficients confidence intervals).

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: general-purpose countGLM() fits four families selects best user-chosen criterion (decide = "BIC" default; also accepts "AIC", "LogLik", "McFadden"), annotated dispersion zero-inflation diagnostics.

previous_headings: Package functions
title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: summarizeCountData(formula, data) — Produces numerical summaries (mean, variance, dispersion ratio, zero count) plot count response predictors. plot type adapts number type predictors (histogram, scatter, violin, heatmap, etc.). GGally::ggpairs() plot also returned.

previous_headings: Package functions
title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: individual fitter returns exponentiated coefficients 95% Wald confidence intervals, randomized quantile residuals, Pearson dispersion ratio, two-panel diagnostic plot (fitted values vs. RQR | normal Q-Q RQR). poissonGLM(formula, data) — Poisson regression via stats::glm. negbinGLM(formula, data) — Negative binomial regression via MASS::glm.nb. zeroinflPoissonGLM(formula, data, ziformula) — Zero-inflated Poisson via pscl::zeroinfl. Separate coefficient tables count zero-inflation components. zeroinflNegbinGLM(formula, data, ziformula) — Zero-inflated negative binomial via pscl::zeroinfl.

previous_headings: Package functions
title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: countGLM(formula, data, ziformula, decide = "BIC") — Fits four families, selects best criterion decide ("BIC", "AIC", "LogLik", "McFadden"), returns plain-language recommendation informed Pearson dispersion ratio observed vs. expected zero counts.

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: model fitters compute: Randomized quantile residuals (RQR) — well-fitting model, RQRs approximately standard normal. diagnostic plot shows fitted values (checking structure) normal Q-Q plot. Pearson dispersion ratio — Pearson chi-squared divided residual degrees freedom. Values near 1 consistent assumed model; values substantially 1 (rule thumb: > 1.2 Poisson fit) suggest overdispersion motivate switching negative binomial /zero-inflated model. Sample size conditions — package checks dataset enough observations relative number predictors; warning issued effective sample--parameter ratio low support reliable estimation. Variance Inflation Factors (VIFs) — VIFs computed predictor flag multicollinearity. Predictors VIF 5 flagged warning users can investigate correlated predictors interpreting coefficients.

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: coefficients reported response scale (exponentiated). count component model, exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change expected count one-unit increase predictor, adjusting simultaneous linear changes predictors. example, value 1.5 means 50% higher expected count. zero-inflation component ZI models, exponentiated coefficient gives multiplicative change odds structural zero.

previous_headings: Coefficient interpretation
title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
text: interpret_coef(model, predictor, component = "count") generates plain-language sentence single coefficient. works model types returned package: Key behaviours: Offset-aware — model includes offset, outcome phrase becomes “expected rate y per unit exposure” rather expected count. Zero-inflated models — pass component = "count" (default) count sub-model component = "zero" zero-inflation sub-model. zero component phrase reads “odds structural zero”. countGLM objects — automatically delegates best-fitting model prints message indicating one used. Non-significant note — coefficient’s p-value exceeds 0.05, note appended: “coefficient discernibly different zero (p = …)”. term name passed predictor must match exactly string coefficient table (e.g. "EPAregion2", "EPAregion"). term found, function stops list available terms.
code: interpret_coef(fit, "pctnonwhite10") #> Holding all other predictors constant, a one-unit increase in pctnonwhite10 is #> associated with a 12.4% increase in the expected count of y #> (exp(β) = 1.124, 95% CI: [1.047, 1.207]).

title: glmOJ — Count Regression Modeling
code: # Install from GitHub # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("ojaroker/glmOJ")

what: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
text: Fits four count regression models supported glmOJ (Poisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated Poisson, zero-inflated negative binomial), selects best metric given decide, provides plain-language recommendation informed dispersion zero-inflation diagnostics.

title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
code: countGLM(formula, data, ziformula = NULL, decide = "BIC", ...)

title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
text: formula model formula count component (e.g. y ~ x1 + x2). response must non-negative integer count variable. data data frame containing variables formula (ziformula provided). ziformula one-sided formula zero-inflation component passed zeroinflPoissonGLM() zeroinflNegbinGLM(). NULL (default), right-hand side formula used. decide Character string specifying model-selection criterion. One "BIC" (default), "AIC", "LogLik" (log-likelihood, higher better), "McFadden" (McFadden pseudo-R², higher better). Matching case-insensitive. ... Additional arguments passed individual model fitter.

title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
text: object class "countGLM", list : call matched call. fits named list successfully fitted model objects (poisson, negbin, zeroinfl_poisson, zeroinfl_negbin). model failed converge omitted. Poisson negative binomial fits include diagnostics$zi_test populated DHARMa zero-inflation test run internally. aic_table named numeric vector AICs, sorted ascending. bic_table named numeric vector BICs, sorted ascending. metric_table named numeric vector selection metric values (criterion named decide), sorted best-first. decide normalised (lower-case) name selection criterion actually used. best_model Character name model selected decide. recommendation plain-language character string explaining selection, including criterion value, dispersion context, zero-inflation test results. vif Named numeric vector Variance Inflation Factors main-effect predictors formula (interaction polynomial terms excluded avoid structural-collinearity false positives). NULL fewer two main-effect predictors present. warning issued VIF > 5.

title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
text: Model selection: model best value decide chosen. "AIC" "BIC" model lowest value wins; "LogLik" "McFadden" model highest value wins. AIC BIC always computed displayed regardless decide. decide = "McFadden", intercept-null models fitted family compute pseudo-R². Zero-inflation diagnostics: DHARMa simulation tests run Poisson negative binomial fits. Results appear result$fits$poisson$diagnostics$zi_test result$fits$negbin$diagnostics$zi_test, inform recommendation text. Individual model warnings suppressed; recommendation summarises findings instead. Individual models support print(), summary(), plot().

title: Fit and compare count regression models — countGLM
code: df <- data.frame(   y  = c(0L, 1L, 2L, 3L, 5L, 0L, 2L, 4L, 1L, 3L),   x1 = c(1.2, -0.4, 0.8, -1.1, 2.0, 0.3, -0.9, 1.5, -0.2, 0.7) ) result <- countGLM(y ~ x1, data = df)           # default: BIC #> Note: zero-inflation component uses the same predictors as the count component (ziformula = NULL). Use `ziformula` to specify a different formula. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. result <- countGLM(y ~ x1, data = df, decide = "AIC") #> Note: zero-inflation component uses the same predictors as the count component (ziformula = NULL). Use `ziformula` to specify a different formula. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. result <- countGLM(y ~ x1, data = df, decide = "McFadden") #> Note: zero-inflation component uses the same predictors as the count component (ziformula = NULL). Use `ziformula` to specify a different formula. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 2 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (2.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. print(result) #>  #> Call: #> countGLM(formula = y ~ x1, data = df, decide = "McFadden") #>  #> Model comparison (sorted by McFadden R2 (descending)): #>             model   AIC   BIC McFadden R2 #>  zeroinfl_poisson 41.43 42.64      0.0710 #>   zeroinfl_negbin 43.43 44.94      0.0710 #>           poisson 39.02 39.63      0.0459 #>            negbin 41.02 41.93      0.0407 #>  #> Selected model: zeroinfl_poisson #>  #> Recommendation: #>   Zero-Inflated Poisson was selected by McFadden R² (McFadden R² = #>   0.0710). The Poisson dispersion ratio is 1.17, consistent with #>   equidispersion. No significant zero-inflation detected (Poisson p = #>   0.890, Negative Binomial p = 0.878). #>  summary(result) #> Summary of selected model (zeroinfl_poisson): #>  #>  #> Call: #> zeroinflPoissonGLM(formula = formula, data = data, ziformula = ziformula) #>  #> Model family: zeroinflPoissonGLM  #>  #> Count component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   2.0801   1.2018   3.6002  0.0089    ** #>           x1   1.3932   0.9095   2.1342  0.1275       #>  #> Zero-inflation component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   0.1201   0.0071   2.0295  0.1417       #>           x1   1.7533   0.2286  13.4471  0.5891       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 1.1578 #> AIC: 41.43

what: glmOJ: Toolkit for Count Regression Modeling — glmOJ
title: glmOJ: Toolkit for Count Regression Modeling — glmOJ
text: Provides workflow fitting, diagnosing, interpreting count regression models. Supports Poisson, negative binomial, zero-inflated Poisson, zero-inflated negative binomial models. Includes data summarization tools, randomized quantile residual diagnostics, general-purpose wrapper (countGLM()) fits four model families selects best AIC.

title: glmOJ: Toolkit for Count Regression Modeling — glmOJ
text: Maintainer: Oscar Jaroker ojaroker@colgate.edu

what: Interpret a model coefficient in natural language — interpret_coef
title: Interpret a model coefficient in natural language — interpret_coef
text: Generates plain-language interpretation exponentiated regression coefficient: percent change expected count (rate, offset present), 95% Wald confidence interval. zero-inflated models, component argument selects count zero-inflation part. note appended whenever coefficient discernibly different zero (p > 0.05).

title: Interpret a model coefficient in natural language — interpret_coef
code: interpret_coef(model, predictor, component = "count")

title: Interpret a model coefficient in natural language — interpret_coef
text: model fitted model returned poissonGLM(), negbinGLM(), zeroinflPoissonGLM(), zeroinflNegbinGLM(), countGLM(). countGLM, best-fitting model used automatically. predictor Character; exact term name appears coefficient table (e.g. "pctnonwhite10", "EPAregion2"). component zero-inflated models: "count" (default) count component, "zero" zero-inflation component. Ignored Poisson negative binomial models.

title: Interpret a model coefficient in natural language — interpret_coef
text: interpretation string, printed console returned invisibly.

what: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
text: Fits negative binomial GLM (via MASS::glm.nb()) returns model coefficients response scale (exponentiated), randomized quantile residuals (RQR), Pearson dispersion ratio, diagnostic plots.

title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
code: negbinGLM(formula, data, assessZeroInflation = TRUE, ...)

title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
text: formula model formula (e.g. y ~ x1 + x2). response must non-negative integer count variable. data data frame containing variables formula. assessZeroInflation Logical; TRUE (default), runs DHARMa simulation-based zero-inflation test fitting. Issues warning significant zero-inflation detected adds zi_test returned diagnostics. Set FALSE calling countGLM(), performs zero-inflation assessment. ... Additional arguments passed MASS::glm.nb().

title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
text: object class c("negbinGLM", "countGLMfit"), list : call matched call. model underlying MASS::glm.nb fit object. summary result summary() fitted model. theta estimated negative binomial dispersion parameter (smaller values indicate overdispersion). coefficients data frame columns term, exp.coef, lower.95, upper.95 (response/exponentiated scale). diagnostics list : rqr Numeric vector randomized quantile residuals. dispersion_ratio Pearson chi-squared / df.residual. plot Patchwork ggplot: fitted vs RQR histo-QQ. r2_plot Squared Pearson residuals vs fitted values. zi_test assessZeroInflation = TRUE, list detected (logical), p_value (numeric), plot (ggplot histogram DHARMa simulated zero proportions vs observed). NULL assessZeroInflation = FALSE. aic AIC fitted model. bic BIC fitted model.

title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
text: Coefficient interpretation: Negative binomial regression models log expected count. Exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change expected count one-unit increase predictor. use: Negative binomial appropriate count data show overdispersion (variance > mean). Pearson dispersion ratio poissonGLM() substantially 1 (rule thumb: > 1.5) common signal. negative binomial adds free parameter theta model extra variance. zero-inflation also detected, consider zeroinflNegbinGLM().

title: Fit a negative binomial regression model — negbinGLM
code: df <- data.frame(   y  = c(0L, 1L, 2L, 3L, 5L, 0L, 2L, 4L, 1L, 3L),   x1 = c(1.2, -0.4, 0.8, -1.1, 2.0, 0.3, -0.9, 1.5, -0.2, 0.7) ) fit <- negbinGLM(y ~ x1, data = df) #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: iteration limit reached #> Warning: iteration limit reached print(fit) #>  #> Call: #> negbinGLM(formula = y ~ x1, data = df) #>  #> Model family: negbinGLM  #>  #> Coefficients (on response scale): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   1.7989   1.0683   3.0291  0.0272     * #>           x1   1.3396   0.8571   2.0936  0.1994       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 1.1657 #> AIC: 41.02 plot(fit)

what: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
title: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
text: Internal plotting function called summarizeCountData. Selects appropriate ggplot based number type predictors supplied.

title: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
code: plot_count_data(y, mf, pred_vars, pred_types)

title: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
text: y Numeric vector non-negative integer counts (response). mf model frame returned model.frame. pred_vars Character vector predictor variable names plot. two names supplied, first two used warning issued. pred_types Named character vector classifying element pred_vars either "continuous" "categorical".

title: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
text: ggplot object.

title: Plot count data against predictors — plot_count_data
text: See summarizeCountData full description plot type produced predictor combination.

what: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
text: Fits Poisson GLM returns model coefficients response scale (exponentiated), randomized quantile residuals (RQR), Pearson dispersion ratio, diagnostic plots.

title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
code: poissonGLM(formula, data, assessZeroInflation = TRUE, ...)

title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
text: formula model formula (e.g. y ~ x1 + x2). response must non-negative integer count variable. data data frame containing variables formula. assessZeroInflation Logical; TRUE (default), runs DHARMa simulation-based zero-inflation test fitting. Issues warning significant zero-inflation detected adds zi_test returned diagnostics. Set FALSE calling countGLM(), performs zero-inflation assessment. ... Additional arguments passed stats::glm().

title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
text: object class c("poissonGLM", "countGLMfit"), list : call matched call. model underlying stats::glm fit object. summary result summary() fitted model. coefficients data frame columns term, exp.coef, lower.95, upper.95 (response/exponentiated scale). diagnostics list : rqr Numeric vector randomized quantile residuals. dispersion_ratio Pearson chi-squared / df.residual. plot Patchwork ggplot: fitted vs RQR histo-QQ. r2_plot Squared Pearson residuals vs fitted values. zi_test assessZeroInflation = TRUE, list detected (logical), p_value (numeric), plot (ggplot histogram DHARMa simulated zero proportions vs observed). NULL assessZeroInflation = FALSE. aic AIC fitted model. bic BIC fitted model.

title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
text: Coefficient interpretation: Poisson regression models log expected count. Exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change expected count one-unit increase predictor. Condition checking: Inspect diagnostics$dispersion_ratio (near 1 good) diagnostics$zi_test$p_value zero-inflation.

title: Fit a Poisson regression model — poissonGLM
code: df <- data.frame(   y  = c(0L, 1L, 2L, 3L, 5L, 0L, 2L, 4L, 1L, 3L),   x1 = c(1.2, -0.4, 0.8, -1.1, 2.0, 0.3, -0.9, 1.5, -0.2, 0.7) ) fit <- poissonGLM(y ~ x1, data = df) #> Warning: Count component: 8 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (8.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. print(fit) #>  #> Call: #> poissonGLM(formula = y ~ x1, data = df) #>  #> Model family: poissonGLM  #>  #> Coefficients (on response scale): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   1.7989   1.0683   3.0290  0.0272     * #>           x1   1.3396   0.8572   2.0936  0.1993       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 1.1658 #> AIC: 39.02 plot(fit)

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
text: Produces numerical graphical summary count response variable, plot automatically adapting number type predictors formula. pairs plot always returned alongside main plot.

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
code: summarizeCountData(formula, data)

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
text: formula formula form y ~ x1 + x2 + ... y non-negative integer count response. Offsets via offset() supported. data data frame containing variables formula.

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
text: named list four elements: summary one-row data.frame containing: mean, var, var_mean_ratio, n_zero, n_total. counts data.frame columns count freq giving frequency observed count value. plot ggplot object. plot type depends number type predictors - see Details. pairs_plot ggpairs object showing pairwise relationships among response predictors. response treated continuous 10 unique values, categorical (factor) otherwise.

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
text: graphical summary chosen based predictors formula: predictors Histogram count response. One continuous predictor Scatter plot loess smooth. One categorical predictor Violin plot jittered points. Two continuous predictors 2D bin plot (geom_bin2d) viridis fill scale. Two categorical predictors Tile heatmap mean counts. One continuous, one categorical predictor Scatter plot loess smooths coloured categorical variable. Three predictors warning issued first two predictors used main plot. pairs plot includes predictors. var_mean_ratio summary table variance--mean ratio. value close 1 consistent Poisson distribution; values substantially greater 1 suggest overdispersion.

title: Summarize count data — summarizeCountData
code: set.seed(1) df <- data.frame(   y  = rpois(100, lambda = 3),   x1 = rnorm(100),   x2 = sample(c("A", "B"), 100, replace = TRUE) )  # No predictors summarizeCountData(y ~ 1, data = df) #> $summary #>   mean     var var_mean_ratio n_zero n_total #> 1 3.05 2.14899      0.7045869      2     100 #>  #> $counts #>   count freq #> 1     0    2 #> 2     1   11 #> 3     2   28 #> 4     3   20 #> 5     4   23 #> 6     5   12 #> 7     6    3 #> 8     8    1 #>  #> $plot  #>  #> $pairs_plot  #>   # One continuous predictor summarizeCountData(y ~ x1, data = df) #> $summary #>   mean     var var_mean_ratio n_zero n_total #> 1 3.05 2.14899      0.7045869      2     100 #>  #> $counts #>   count freq #> 1     0    2 #> 2     1   11 #> 3     2   28 #> 4     3   20 #> 5     4   23 #> 6     5   12 #> 7     6    3 #> 8     8    1 #>  #> $plot #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'  #>  #> $pairs_plot #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.  #>   # One categorical predictor summarizeCountData(y ~ x2, data = df) #> $summary #>   mean     var var_mean_ratio n_zero n_total #> 1 3.05 2.14899      0.7045869      2     100 #>  #> $counts #>   count freq #> 1     0    2 #> 2     1   11 #> 3     2   28 #> 4     3   20 #> 5     4   23 #> 6     5   12 #> 7     6    3 #> 8     8    1 #>  #> $plot  #>  #> $pairs_plot  #>   # Mixed predictors summarizeCountData(y ~ x1 + x2, data = df) #> $summary #>   mean     var var_mean_ratio n_zero n_total #> 1 3.05 2.14899      0.7045869      2     100 #>  #> $counts #>   count freq #> 1     0    2 #> 2     1   11 #> 3     2   28 #> 4     3   20 #> 5     4   23 #> 6     5   12 #> 7     6    3 #> 8     8    1 #>  #> $plot #> `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'  #>  #> $pairs_plot #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`. #> `stat_bin()` using `bins = 30`. Pick better value `binwidth`.  #>

what: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
text: Fits zero-inflated negative binomial model (via pscl::zeroinfl()) separate count zero-inflation components. Returns coefficients response scale, randomized quantile residuals, dispersion ratio, diagnostic plot.

title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
code: zeroinflNegbinGLM(formula, data, ziformula = NULL, ...)

title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
text: formula model formula count component (e.g. y ~ x1 + x2). response must non-negative integer count. data data frame containing variables formula (ziformula provided). ziformula one-sided formula zero-inflation component (e.g. ~ x1). NULL (default), right-hand side formula used components. Use ~ 1 intercept-zero-inflation model. ... Additional arguments passed pscl::zeroinfl().

title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
text: object class c("zeroinflNegbinGLM", "zeroinflGLMfit", "countGLMfit"), list : call matched call. model underlying pscl::zeroinfl fit object. theta estimated negative binomial dispersion parameter. coefficients list two data frames, columns term, exp.coef, lower.95, upper.95: count Exponentiated coefficients count component. zero Exponentiated coefficients zero-inflation component. summary result summary() fitted model. diagnostics list rqr, dispersion_ratio, plot (see poissonGLM() details). aic AIC fitted model. bic BIC fitted model.

title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
text: Coefficient interpretation: Count component: exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change expected count among non-structural-zero observations, one-unit increase predictor, adjusting simultaneous linear changes predictors. example, 1.5 means 50% higher expected count. Zero component: exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change odds structural zero (vs. entering count process) one-unit increase predictor. use: Zero-inflated negative binomial flexible four models — handles excess zeros overdispersion non-zero counts. Prefer zeroinflPoissonGLM() non-zero counts remain overdispersed zero-inflation accounted .

title: Fit a zero-inflated negative binomial regression model — zeroinflNegbinGLM
code: df <- data.frame(   y  = c(0L, 0L, 0L, 1L, 2L, 0L, 3L, 0L, 1L, 0L),   x1 = c(1.2, -0.4, 0.8, -1.1, 2.0, 0.3, -0.9, 1.5, -0.2, 0.7) ) fit <- zeroinflNegbinGLM(y ~ x1, data = df) #> Warning: Count component: 4 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (4.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 6 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (6.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. print(fit) #>  #> Call: #> zeroinflNegbinGLM(formula = y ~ x1, data = df) #>  #> Model family: zeroinflNegbinGLM  #>  #> Count component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   1.3408   0.4922   3.6523  0.5663       #>           x1   0.9433   0.4383   2.0304  0.8814       #>  #> Zero-inflation component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   0.6737   0.0820   5.5325  0.7131       #>           x1   2.1030   0.4077  10.8486  0.3745       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 2.0053 #> AIC: 31.60 plot(fit)   # Intercept-only zero component: fit2 <- zeroinflNegbinGLM(y ~ x1, data = df, ziformula = ~ 1) #> Warning: Count component: 4 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (4.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended.

what: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
text: Fits zero-inflated Poisson model (via pscl::zeroinfl()) separate count zero-inflation components. Returns coefficients response scale, randomized quantile residuals, dispersion ratio, diagnostic plot.

title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
code: zeroinflPoissonGLM(formula, data, ziformula = NULL, ...)

title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
text: formula model formula count component (e.g. y ~ x1 + x2). response must non-negative integer count. data data frame containing variables formula (ziformula provided). ziformula one-sided formula zero-inflation component (e.g. ~ x1). NULL (default), right-hand side formula used components. Use ~ 1 intercept-zero-inflation model. ... Additional arguments passed pscl::zeroinfl().

title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
text: object class c("zeroinflPoissonGLM", "zeroinflGLMfit", "countGLMfit"), list : call matched call. model underlying pscl::zeroinfl fit object. coefficients list two data frames, columns term, exp.coef, lower.95, upper.95: count Exponentiated coefficients count component. zero Exponentiated coefficients zero-inflation component. summary result summary() fitted model. diagnostics list rqr, dispersion_ratio, plot (see poissonGLM() details). aic AIC fitted model. bic BIC fitted model.

title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
text: Coefficient interpretation: Count component: exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change expected count among non-structural-zero observations, one-unit increase predictor, adjusting simultaneous linear changes predictors. example, 1.5 means 50% higher expected count. Zero component: exponentiating coefficient gives multiplicative change odds structural zero (vs. entering count process) one-unit increase predictor. value greater 1 means higher odds structural zero. use: Zero-inflated Poisson appropriate zeros standard Poisson model predict (excess zeros), non-zero counts follow Poisson distribution. non-zero counts also overdispersed, prefer zeroinflNegbinGLM().

title: Fit a zero-inflated Poisson regression model — zeroinflPoissonGLM
code: df <- data.frame(   y  = c(0L, 0L, 0L, 1L, 2L, 0L, 3L, 0L, 1L, 0L),   x1 = c(1.2, -0.4, 0.8, -1.1, 2.0, 0.3, -0.9, 1.5, -0.2, 0.7) ) fit <- zeroinflPoissonGLM(y ~ x1, data = df) #> Warning: Count component: 4 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (4.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended. #> Warning: Zero-inflation component: 6 zeros for 1 predictor(s) (6.0 per predictor). At least 10 zeros per ZI predictor is recommended. print(fit) #>  #> Call: #> zeroinflPoissonGLM(formula = y ~ x1, data = df) #>  #> Model family: zeroinflPoissonGLM  #>  #> Count component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   1.3408   0.4923   3.6521  0.5662       #>           x1   0.9433   0.4383   2.0303  0.8814       #>  #> Zero-inflation component (exponentiated coefficients): #>         term exp.coef lower.95 upper.95 p.value stars #>  (Intercept)   0.6737   0.0821   5.5318  0.7132       #>           x1   2.1030   0.4077  10.8484  0.3745       #>  #> Dispersion ratio: 1.6711 #> AIC: 29.60 plot(fit)   # Intercept-only zero component: fit2 <- zeroinflPoissonGLM(y ~ x1, data = df, ziformula = ~ 1) #> Warning: Count component: 4 events (y > 0) for 1 predictor(s) (4.0 per predictor). At least 10 events per predictor is recommended.
